content:
The dorm was quiet, save for the soft shuffle of paper and the faint clink of glass jars. Late afternoon sunlight slanted through the dorm windows, painting long bars of gold across the floor. Dust motes drifted lazily in the air, catching in the glow. Sol knelt by his bed, sleeves rolled and hair tied loosely back as he worked through what looked like the most serious mission of the week: *packing*.

Sol was surrounded by half-open drawers, unrolled ribbons, and a precarious pile of paints, brushes, and food that looked like it might stage a rebellion if left unattended. His hands shimmered faintly with golden light as objects floated in slow orbit around him.

A loaf of bread bobbed toward a wicker picnic basket lined in red-checked cloth, followed by neatly wrapped sandwiches, slices of fruit, and jars of jam. A wedge of cheese drifted uncertainly before he frowned at it. “No… Caspian’ll eat half the basket anyway, better double that.”

With another wave of his hand, two more sandwiches and an additional cheese wheel obediently floated in.

Across from him, a larger satchel waited, already stuffed with what looked like the supply closet of an entire art school. Brushes, palettes, watercolor tins, and neatly labeled acrylic paints nestled beside rolls of parchment, sketchbooks, ribbons, yarn, and a small container of glitter that shimmered faintly, as if it *knew* it was trouble.

“Okay…” Sol muttered, ticking off items on his fingers. “Food, art supplies, possible chaos. Perfect.”

He snapped his fingers, sending the last few items drifting neatly into their bags, except for a pear, which bonked him on the head mid-flight.

He blinked, rubbing the spot with a mock glare. “Excuse *you*. That’s assault.” Then, with a laugh, he plucked the offending fruit from the air and tucked it safely into the basket.

He started to rise, brushing off his pants, and then he froze. “…The coffee and tea.”

Spinning on his heel, he nearly tripped as he lunged for tins of black tea and espresso on the nightstand. He caught it with a triumphant little noise, holding it aloft like a prize. “You thought you could escape, didn’t you?”

He set it carefully on top of the packed basket, surveying his work with the sort of proud smile that came from pure, hard-earned relief. Everything was ready now: sandwiches, fruit, drinks, a blanket folded neatly at the bottom, and an artist’s worth of supplies in the satchel. Enough for all five of them, plus seconds for anyone who was still hungry.

“Alright,” Sol murmured, brushing a lock of hair from his eyes. “Snacks for five, art for ten, and hopefully no explosions.”

He lifted the basket into the crook of his arm, slung the satchel over his shoulder, and took one last look at the golden light flooding his room. For a fleeting moment, it felt peaceful, almost *normal*.

He stepped into the hall, calling brightly toward the common room,
“Alright, everyone! I’m declaring a *mandatory break* before any of us lose what’s left of our sanity! I’ve got food, drinks, paints, and glitter, and I’m not afraid to use them!

His voice carried a laugh at the end, half challenge, half invitation, before he took a seat on the couch.

<@128691687169064961> <@206175579358232578> <@278357187246555137> <@544474001288986624>
[open] The Small Moments That Matter

As was tradition when in regard to the draconic dynasty of the Vanori, Vornakir was more often than not the first to present himself when summons were given or presence was requested. Today was no different as the sound of two locks clicking open sounded off from the first door on the right down the hall, and out walked the sovereign himself. Much like his companion doing the summons, his hair was uncharacteristically messily tied back in a lazy half bun. On average, he presented himself with full pride and groomed effects such as pressed clothing pristine of soiling, and his hair brushed and combed out, perfectly lush for the sassiest of hair flips when he needed to punctuate a statement. It was custom, according to him, to always be seen at his best. 

However as the days dragged on and on, he began to tame the metaphoric beast of his pride and allowed his friends to see a more real side of him. Such as today. A simple black shirt trimmed with golden lines that helped shape and accentuate the figure beneath the top, of course with a surgically removed left sleeve sewn shut as a harrowing reminder of his lack. 

Strolling into the room like the entire campus belonged to him, and everyone attending it his dutiful citizens as he had done each time he entered literally any room, he turned those glass petals of auric hues onto Sol with a raised brow cocked in his regard. 

“And a fine morning to you as well, Sol. Pardon my lack of decorum but I’d be remiss if I didn’t ask for clarity — did you mean to imply you would use food, drinks, paints and glitter *on* us all? You’re quite a charmer and your particular brand of masculine beauty is of course only second to mine but I must remind you that I duel for a different league.” He quipped in clear and obvious jest judging by the easy smile he managed to offer in turn. “But I’ll let you know if my alignment changes. However if you’re offering to share these things with us, I may indulge you.”

Despite the banter and light ribbing, he felt no shame nor concern with approaching and sitting at Sol’s side in the couch. He always opted for sitting in the couch, it was the only seat in the commons that had a decent view of every possible avenue a potential threat could enter.

“May I ask what made you desire an arts, crafts and treats day?” He inquired, leaning more comfortably back into the couch’s cushion and of course since Sol sat to his left, he didn’t have to worry about elbows bumping uncomfortably. ((*Looks at the camera*)). A faint aroma of jasmine and mint lifted from him and mingled with the delicate scents of food Sol had prepared for them all, indicating he was likely up early anyway and chose to bathe this morning.

Sol blinked before laughter bubbled up, soft but genuine. He shook his head, brushing a loose strand of hair out of his face. “Second most beautiful, huh? I’ll take the compliment… even if it’s wrapped in ego.”

He leaned back against the the couch, still balancing the basket in one arm. “And no, not *on* you, your Majesty. I promise the glitter is for artistic purposes only, unless you keep talking like that, in which case, I might test how much sparkle dragon scales can handle.” The corner of his mouth quirked up in a grin that was all teasing and comfort.

It matched the rest of him matched his tone today; unusually casual and unguarded. Sol’s black-and-yellow flannel shirt was rolled to the elbows; underneath, he wore a deep-black V-neck and ripped jeans, favoring comfort over the polish he usually wore like armor. Even the brown hiking boots looked suspiciously new, chosen more for their appearance than for the trail. The silver and gold glinting from his wrists and ears remained, but his thin-framed glasses softened him, giving him an almost scholarly vibe.

When Vornakir sat next to Sol, the faint cinnamon-chai scent of the basket mingled pleasantly with the jasmine and mint that followed the drow like perfume.

“Arts, crafts, and treats day,” he repeated, half-smiling at the name like it had only just occurred to him how ridiculous it sounded out loud. “Because I figured we could use something that didn’t involve dueling, bleeding, or saving the world before dinner.”

His gaze drifted briefly toward the far wall, eyes dimming in thought. “Art has this way of pulling people together. It doesn’t matter your class, your title, or which court you serve. It’s… connection. And after everything lately…” He trailed off, thinking of the tension that had crept between Caspian and Zašia, of words left unsaid and wounds no one quite knew how to patch.

Then he exhaled, shaking off the heaviness with a small smile. “Besides,” he said, leaning forward to pull open the basket, revealing a glimmer of color and carefully wrapped food, “we could all use a little distraction. And when was the last time all of us did anything fun together?”

He lifted a brow toward him and held his hand dramatically to his heart, playful again. “Unless you’re telling me the mighty Vornakir Vanori doesn’t know his way around a paintbrush. That would be truly tragic.”

Vornakir playfully drew his hand up toward his face to feign offense. “Ego? Me, the Obsidian Prince of the Barricade and first Golden Scaled One in centuries destined for the Dragonbone Crown worn only by those born to the scales? How dare you insinuate something such as that.” 

Nonetheless he leaned slightly away from Sol at the mention of glitter on scale violence. “Of all the threats made on my life, none compare to the inconvenience that’s born from trying to clean anything fine and small from ‘neath my scales.” That much, though was said in good faith and comfort in turn was genuine. Just the thought of trying to get specks of glitter out from between his overlapping naturally grown scales exhausted him. 

Ochre stained reptilian eyes suddenly flicked down to stare at the basket as if it had come to life suddenly and had become an adversary. “Mmh, treats?” He then moved to stare through Sol. Appraising him, studying him like his life depended on it — because it did. Surely he’d known by now Vornakir rarely ever indulged in another person’s handiwork be it food or beverage. Especially if he didn’t personally watch the entire time. 

“Erhh you must forgive me if I don’t indulge too much in the picnic portion of what you’ve prepared - at least until I’ve seen enough of the others do so and survive. I know it may seem excessive to you and perhaps foreign, even outlandish that someone would ever conceive the idea to do this to me…” ((Looks at the camera))

He gestured then to Sol and the basket flippantly. “But this would not be the first time I’ve been nearly assassinated via poison if you wanted me dead. We hardly know one another so I haven’t figured out if I can trust you enough to prepare food outside of my eye-line and then eat it. Of course I know if you wanted me dead there have been ample opportunities but. I find it hard to trust people, so I only ask for a bit of patience in that regard.”

He cleared his throat, clearly wanting to connect with his peers but he still unfortunately was the type that needed faith and trust to proven and earned before it was given from him. “However, I resent the notion I don’t know my way around a brush. Have you forgotten I have dozens of finished pieces of art just waiting to be shown off?” He reminded him, trying to keep the mood light despite the small turn toward distrust that had been shown before.

Sol blinked, caught off guard for half a second, then his expression gentled. He hadn’t meant to step on a trust landmine, and the moment Vornakir explained himself, the pieces clicked together easily enough.

“Hey,” Sol said softly, holding up his hands in mock surrender, a crooked smile tugging at his mouth. “No offense taken. Honestly, I should’ve remembered, not everyone grew up in a house where ‘sharing a meal’ could come with half a dozen threats to your life.”

He reached for the basket and propped the lid open again, pushing it toward the center of the table between them. “You don’t have to eat anything you’re not comfortable with. Most of it’s still sealed.” He held up a jar of fruit preserves, the wax seal intact, then a wrapped bundle of bread. “But if it’ll help, I can remake your portion right here. I’ve got extra ingredients, and I don’t mind proving I didn’t lace the sandwiches with slow-acting assassination glitter.”

He quietly laughed. “Trust me, if I’d wanted you dead, I’d have found something much more poetic. Maybe enchant an actor to follow you around and monologue your flaws until you give up out of sheer irritation.”

The teasing softened as he leaned forward, resting his forearms on his knees. “You’re right, though; we don’t know each other *that* well yet. Not like I’d like to. So let’s fix that.” He gestured loosely to the art supply bag near his feet. “You’ve got your paintings, I’ve got brushes and blank canvases. Bring one of your pieces to the picnic. Show me something you’re proud of. I’d love to see what your world looks like when it’s not wrapped in duty, titles, and fighting to survive.”

He smiled, open and easy again, the earlier sting already smoothed away. “If you trust me enough to come paint, I’ll count that as progress.”

A single hand, regal though he may be the manner in which he gestured was gentle, raised in Sol’s direction in turn. “It’s my problem, I can acknowledge that much so don’t fret too much. You need not go out of your way on my behalf. You’ve done plenty trying to get us all together to wind down.” He added with a crinkle to his nose as he passed a genuine smile in Sol’s direction. 

“For the time being I’m not too hungry anyway but I’m sure once I see others enjoying what you prepared without succumbing to an unseen poison, I’ll be fine to trust that you aren’t making an attempt on my life.” He assured with a calm nod while eyeballing the basket warily still.

However he could also easily acknowledge that things got a little awkward so he tried to lighten the mood with a jest. “I commend your effort on the hypothetical, but I must burst your glittery bubble. It could follow me for only a mere second before it realized I have no flaws to monologue.” He added with a wink and a sassy flip of his hair. 

Where Sol’s magic was warm and elegant, Vornakir’s was efficient and disciplined - noble in its own way as he snapped his fingers and flourished his hand lazily to conjure his mage hand. A shower of brilliant auric sparks danced in the air and formed his mystic expert mage hand - the arcane extension of his body and will he mastered so long ago. 

As it glided through the air, it went toward his room - opening the door to fetch his paintings presumably. “I would love to paint with you, and show you my work. Unfortunately a couple of years ago, I lost my muse for the passion and my spark has since dulled a little. I’ve painted since, but it’s been quite some time since something truly burned passionately in me so deeply that I couldn’t stop myself from immortalizing it on canv— No, not that one. The next shelf, yes there. Bundle them all up.” He called over his shoulder as if he was aware of exactly what the sentient hand was doing.

Moments later, it would return into the room carrying with it like a drone delivering a payload a rather thick cloth sack obtuse in shape as if it held a dozen canvases of similar size.

“And I would appreciate you allowing me to know you better as well.”

Sol’s eyes tracked the mage hand as it drifted across the room in a cascade of golden light, his lips parting in a quiet sound of awe. “Show-off,” he said softly, though the teasing edge melted into something almost admiring. “Even your spells have posture.”

He shifted slightly on the couch as he watched the hand disappear down the hall. The air shimmered faintly where the sparks had hung a moment ago, and he couldn’t help but smile. “You make magic look so easy,” he smiled, more to himself than to Vornakir. “All that precision, every deliberate gesture. I’d paint *that* if I could capture it right.”

When the mage hand returned, burdened with the sack, Sol leaned forward, eying it inquisitively. “You say you’ve lost your muse,” he said quietly, eyes lifting to meet Vornakir’s, “but someone who’s truly lost it doesn’t keep the art close. They throw it away so they don’t have to remember what they loved.” He smiled faintly, almost wistfully. “Keeping it means there’s still a spark, it’s just waiting for air.” His eye fell back to the sack, “So do I get a sneak peek before we go, or what?”

His tone lightened a beat later, grin returning as he sat back. “And as for getting to know each other, I’m an open book.” He tilted his head, glasses slipping slightly down his nose. “So if you have any questions, feel free to ask.”

The smile Vornakir gave was considerably smaller now, indeterminate as to why. He watched the hand wistfully as it floated away. “I wish you had known me before the incident. I know Zašia and perhaps Caspian as well would say I’m just over reacting but. Things have been on a severe downward trend since I lost my arm. Before, that hand was merely a third hand for me working in tandem. It…amplified what I previously excelled at but was never required.”

Somber acceptance. That’s what the smaller smile was. Bitterness to be sure, but acceptance nonetheless. “Now it’s a requirement.” He finished with a sigh as it began to make its way back in but it was replaced with real mirth toward Sol shortly after. “That’d be a sight to see - me, immortalized in art. I can hear my opposition now, at how vain I just be to commission myself in the most brilliant oils and paints by the most talented hands.” This of course implying even in his home at the Barricade he wasn’t in any of the art that adorned the manor he was raised in.

He took the sack from his mage hand and dismissed it, the hand shimmering of existence in a shower of lovely silent sparks. Though he didn’t speak on the topic of his muse any further, he did unwrap the bag and pull a completely random couple pieces of canvas out to display to Sol.

“But of course.” The first one was haunting but beautiful in its own right. Caverns spreading endlessly with stonework buildings dotting the landscape. Twinkling lights lit in some windows, others blacked out for the evening. These details made up the far edges of the painting, framing the centerpiece in perspective. A massive tree, shrouded in secrecy but emanating this mysterious aura that begged for closer study yet warned against getting too close. Its gargantuan stature elicited awe but folded into the beauty was a drowning sorrow. 

Not quite a tree, its trunk gray and rigid like the world's largest stalagmite. Not quite stone, its branches hanging and occasionally swaying like a giant's arms in a non-existent captured still frame of breeze. Every indention in the bark was a story of Drow culture long forgotten, every branch a family in the history of the barricade, every root a source of power and pride for its peoples. This tree was more than just some oddity grown in a monstrous cavern beneath the surface - It was their most history. It was their future. It was their most sacred secret. 

"Nythrilith, the Black Bloom." Vornakir answered an unspoken question he expected to be asked while he was busy pulling a second one out - This one at first glance being pulled from the bag was of a person. A woman?

The door down the hall swung open and Caspian emerged stretching and stifling a yawn. The fighter is dressed comfortably, a light sweater over a loose tunic and pants. His blond hair appears combed and carefully styled in a much less formal manner. Caspian's fashion swings wildly between military dress and workout clothes and this is one of the few times casual can be used to describe the man. He meandered down the hallway not rushing to interrupt the art show occurring in the common room. He stepped into the kitchen opens every kitchen cabinet examining the contents before eventually settling on a bag of trail mix he finds and he began pecking at it leaving half the cabinets open behind him. After a moment he glanced back and quickly backtracks to shut them. The flow of nuts, pretzels, and small candy is a constant from the bag to his mouth as he finally joins the other two. 

He nodded to Sunni and looked intently at the packed picnic basket nearby. He used a hand to lift the lid and peak at what's contained inside. 

"Ooooooh... snacks plus some arts and crafts? That's fun. I could use a break."

He then moved on to observe the painting of the cavern and tree. His brow furrowed as he examines the brushwork and colors. Remaining quiet save for the crunching sound as he bit into another few pieces of trail mix. He nodded approvingly after a long several minutes of observation. He looked up blank expression on his face after the nod ended.

"I've never really been good at art. Neither making nor appreciating it really. It seems like a good painting to me but then again most art is to me. I like it Scales its interesting to get an idea of where you grew up. By the way, while I will admit that you've had a difficult few weeks you are no lesser than you were before encountering that beholder.", He admitted to overhearing the last bit of Vornakir and Sunni's conversation with his statement.

He trailed off as he glanced at the next painting. "And what do we have here?"

What *did* we have here? It was leaps and bounds more painstakingly brought to life on this canvas. Where the first piece could be appreciated as just a nice piece of art, this one was easily among if not his best work. Anyone with eyes could see the intense passion, could see the hours on hours that this piece took to paint. 

The thousands of brush strokes that made her skin look so life like it made you want to caress her melancholy cheek. The dozens if not hundreds of hours that was spent slaving over it made her eyes glimmer under the backdrop moon that washed her figure in its light. The love and complete devotion it took to capture every strand of her silver hair that flowed like a river of the precious metal down her shoulders and covering breasts tastefully - bare but nowhere near the focus of this masterpiece. The intimate knowledge that cultivated every curve of her body as she sat in a rippling grotto pool surrounded by live greenery and flowers. 

She was sat in the water with one knee slightly up enough for her to rest her head atop it and the putter outstretched - head turned to face the painter’s point of view with eyes that shone like a pair of precious stones. Her face was so soft, it would bring to mind innocent prey waiting to be hunted if not for the stainless silver scales that adorned her neck and just barely her cheeks - shaping her face more pointed like a predator. The face of a lamb but the fixings of a wolf - or rather, *a dragon*.

As he held it outward for them to inspect at their leisure, it was almost as if he didn’t care about the painting. Purposefully. Forcefully. Like he wanted them to think this was just any other painting that meant as little or as much as the others in the Sack - but a keen eye might notice within the opening of it, the glint of more silver scales. She was the subject of more than one piece clearly. 

“You flatter me, Caspian - yes it would appear it’s arts and crafts and snacks day.” He said, distracting himself from the painting - the back of which was dated roughly three or so years back.

Sol shifted a little closer as he took in the canvases laid out before him. His brows knit slightly in focus. The cavern scene drew him in first. “That’s… incredible,” he said after a quiet moment. “You captured the stillness of the environment so well. While I’m not exactly sure what a Nythrilith is, I can tell it’s sacred base on the way you portrayed it.” His eyes lingered on the faint light in the tree’s roots, the shadow that clung to the stone. “It feels… heavy. Like it’s carrying too many things at once.”

Then came the second piece, and Sol’s expression softened into something almost reverent. He tilted his head, voice lowering. “And her… there’s something about her eyes. Like she’s half in this world and half somewhere else.” He hesitated, then looked up at Vornakir. “Who is she, if you don’t mind me asking.”

When Caspian chimed in, Sol let out a small laugh under his breath. “You don’t have to be good at art to be an artist,” he said, turning just enough to meet his eyes. “You just have to try. It’s not about making something perfect, it’s about feeling something and making it worth remembering.” His smile was small, but genuine. “You’d be surprised what people can create when they stop worrying about whether it’s good.”

His attention drifted back to the paintings, quieter now. “They’re beautiful, Vornakir. Both of them,” he said, voice thoughtful. “Even if you think your spark’s gone, I can see it all over these.”

He glanced between them again, that familiar, grin creeping back in. “But for what it’s worth? I’d love to see more. Maybe today’s as good a day as any to start finding that spark again.”

From the mouth of the hall that emptied into the common spaces, Zašia watched the others mingle. She'd lingered because she'd seen Caspian make his way there ahead of her, and she was still giving him plenty of space. Then, it was the unveiling of Vornakir's art.

At first, she'd been ready to step out to compliment his work, but the woman in the second piece held her in place again. She didn't need to be so close as Sol to see the reverence in the piece.

One step back, then another, all to give *her* the space to wrangle with the sudden twist of a knife in her gut unbidden. Was it jealousy or envy? The two were so close as to make the differences difficult to discern.

She busied herself with the buttons on her sweater. She didn't often wear red, it didn't seem to suit her. It made her fair skin seem paler, detracted from the violet hue of her eyes. It had, however, conveyed the confidence she felt inside of her.

Until that very moment.

*Take a breath, and remember who the* fuck *you are. Second of your name, but second to no one else.*

Zašia emerged with a pleasant smile on her face. "How are my boys today?" she asked as she breezed her way past them all to pour herself some water.

Sol looked up from the couch as Zašia stepped into the room, his smile widening the moment he caught sight of her. The red hit him first; he sat up straighter, eyes bright behind his glasses as he gave a low, appreciative hum.

“Well, that’s a new color on you,” he said, tone half-teasing, half-genuine admiration. “I like it. Red’s a statement color that commands attention, and you wear it like you already know exactly what kind of power you have.”

He gestured idly toward her with one hand, “Confidence suits you, Zašia. And the cut of the sweater is perfect on you; it brings focus up to the shoulders without swallowing your shape.”

He leaned back casually, grin still curling at the corners. “You missed His Royal Highness’s grand gallery reveal, but you’ve got excellent timing. We were just about to head out and turn campus into an art studio slash picnic. You’re more than welcome to join us.”

Vornakir seemed to be ready to open his mouth and remark on the paintings but then at that moment, Zašia arrived and his attention moved to her instead. Not that he hid them away, he was just in the process of putting them back in the bag as she arrived - And he didn't make any effort to conceal them as he stored them for the later reveals. 

"Good morning, darling. Mm? I can't speak for the others but I'm quite fine." He said, giving a gestured in the vague direction of Sol. "And he's got the right of it. Red is a lovely color on you, though I'm partial to violet and of course gold at times. I believe the confidence and aura it exudes from you in this case surpasses that."

After cinching off the bag containing his art work, he leaned back comfortably in his spot on the couch. "It's quite fine, 'His Royal Highness' will show her as well when we're all out and about and everyone can see what I have before we all set in for your painting extravaganza." He said, tasting the playful nickname on his own tongue. 

At least he didn't seem to have any intention of *hiding* whatever the paintings were from Zašia - Surely that meant they were nothing to concern herself over.

Caspian examined the painting of the woman with raised eyebrows. He seemed surprised that the drow prince would have painted something so racy. He chuckles quietly before speaking up,

"Wow, I see you have quite the appreciation for the female form. Thought that something so... suggestive, would be a little improper for someone of your station. Then again who am I to understand how things work in the barricade."

After Sol asked who the painting was of Caspian elbows him a few times jokingly. 

"Do you mean her name because it seems pretty clear that they were..."

He cuts off with a cough as Zašia enters the room and greets them. The two of them still weren't on the best terms but even he wasn't that tactless or cruel. Unfortunately he hadn't realized she was close at hand before entering the room in the first place. Turning to her he gives a terse smile, the echoes of their arguments slipping into his thoughts. In his reply Caspian's tone doesn't hold the same good nature he usually affects, he's not mad still but there is still much left unspoken that needs to be addressed. The end result is polite but very curt for the affable Caspian. 

"Hey Z. Doing fine, you? Apparently we're all going to be doing painting today, you handy with a brush?"

He turned back to see Vornakir wrapping up the paintings. There's a flicker of disappointment to not see any other's that might have remained in his bag. Curiosity simply began to itch the back of his skull as he wondered what else lay within. Thankfully he had plenty more trail mix to dull the awkwardness and curiosity of the moment with.
+5 Development Points

Zašia returned Caspian's smile with one that didn't meet her eyes. Quite so, far too much they need to air, whether with words or through physical action. One way or another, they needed it out of their systems.

"Not as such, no," she answered his question, forcing her expression not to turn sour. Especially not as her gaze flitted to where Vornakir was re-placing the painting.

She couldn't decide if she wanted to scream or throw up. She had no right to the former and the latter seemed a disproportionate response to the situation, but she couldn't convince her gut not to churn. 

"I can see you have a type," she remarked airily to her suitor, the very picture of unruffled pleasantness. There wasn't a trace of her usual vocal fry when she spoke. "I hadn't realized you'd been in a romantic relationship with one of the other scaleborn." Oh, but Zašia was shrewd about some things. Observant. 

She also had been under the impression that he hadn't had any romantic affiliations at all prior to her. Had he lied or misrepresented something to allow her to infer incorrectly about the situation?

Did it really matter?

"You captured her visage beautifully," she complimented genuinely even as she felt a pang inside of her. That one she knew was both Envy *and* her sister Jealousy. Would that anyone should want to do the same for her.

Breathing out, she turned that smile to Sol next. "Thank you." She actually preened. "I don't wear it often — my mother says it washes me out and does nothing for my eyes — but I still like it a great deal. A gift from a distant relative who, incidentally, thought my eyes were gold." A quiet chuckle. "My father's uncle is not known to be the most observant man."

From his usual perch along the kitchen counter, Redd had been quiet through it all—feeding Whimsy from a small pouch of dried fruit, half-listening to Sol’s easy warmth and Caspian’s teasing. The others’ voices hummed around him like distant music, but his eyes were fixed on Vornakir’s hands as the first canvas was revealed.

While everyone was marveling and cooing over the portait of the muse, Redd’s attention stayed with the first painting.

Nythrilith, the Black Bloom.

The cavern swallowed in shadow, the monolithic trunk gleaming like molten stone frozen mid-pulse. There was something reverent in the brushwork—an ache, a warning. And the longer he looked, the more it pressed against a memory he’d tried to bury: Sorjin’s face after the beating. Bruised, blood dried along his lip, eyes still bright with hurt and confusion.

In that moment—morbid and a little cruel—Redd understood where Velvyn had come from. It didn’t excuse her, but it made sense now. Sorjin had thought of the Bloom as something to replicate, to make useful. But here, in Vornakir’s hand, it was clear: this wasn’t utility. It was history, belief, and mourning, all at once. The sense of scared carried even through the painting. 

“It’s even more than what he claimed it to be,” Redd murmured under his breath. He didn’t name Sorjin. Didn’t need to.

When he finally looked up, Vornakir’s gaze had found his—just a flicker, but enough. A quiet understanding passed between them. Redd nodded once, a rare, wordless acknowledgment.

Then he blinked, drew in a slow breath, and forced the heaviness back down. Whimsy hooted softly beside his ear, impatient for another treat.

Redd smirked faintly, straightened, and clapped his hands once. “So,” he said, voice breaking the quiet, “we doing this or what?”

As Vornakir was taking his time putting the painting of the woman away, still providing the obvious proof he wasn't hiding it from Zašia, he took a glance at Caspian and then Sol as the two of them were commenting on the woman in the painting. However, Zašia herself struck the conversation before he could answer them and so he aimed to answer all of them at the same time. 

A brow was cocked in her direction as he cinched the bag and sat back comfortably in his spot on the couch - ready to show them all what else was inside later when the picnic officially began. This was supposed to merely be a sneak peak after all. "A type?" He questioned, as to him comparing the two didn't come out with many similarities other than pale skin and silver hair. That was more coincidence than a type, at least in his eyes. 

But the term 'romantic relationship' twisted into the same sentence as 'Scaleborn' caused his jaw to tick and his lips to purse. "I wasn't," He admitted, "Nyxavera was not my romantic partner - That's her name by the way." He said, shooting his attention to Sol and Caspian albeit briefly. "She and I were close growing up, and there was feelings for a time but." That lingering moment is when his Nobility showed the most. His posture straightened, his chin lifted, his eyes hollowed and his mask was firmly in place. Not to hide emotions, but to show proper composure when speaking of a sore topic. "Scaleborn are forbidden from such things with one another. We are not allowed to have any courtship of any sort considering we're to kill each other in time. I'm sure you can see how that wouldn't make for a good courtship." 

In the time they'd known each other, she could recall *vividly* how extraordinarily intense he allowed his emotions to be when the topic of 'controlling his love life' was brought up. How did he put it to her in their privacy? *'I'll be damned if anyone tells me who I can and cannot love.'*

Suddenly though his attention snapped to Redd. He caught the gaze and returned the nod. Where Redd's was acknowledgement, Vornakir's was a silent respect. He knew what was being referred to. "It's awe inspiring, is it not? Some say Lunathrae herself descended from the heavens and planted it so that her children may thrive." He said in reference to what some here may or may not know as a common Drow belief - Lunathrae the Pale Lady represented by the Moon. A goddess to their people.

Redd had half-turned already, reaching for the picnic basket when Vornakir spoke of Lunathrae. The words stilled him.

It wasn’t a story he knew, but it stirred something familiar all the same. His mother used to tell him tales like that—softly, over the crackle of the hearth—of trees that carried prayers in their rings, of storms that remembered names. He’d grown up in wilds where the air itself felt awake. Listening to Vornakir now, he could hear that same pulse in the Barricade’s caverns: a living power, different from his own forests, but no less immense. His wild druid nature responded to it instinctively, recognizing the quiet grandeur of something that shaped life simply by existing.

His gaze lingered on Nythrilith, the Black Bloom. In the painting, even stone seemed to breathe. Roots, rock, and shadow all thrummed with something ancient—like the world itself had once stopped to listen.

“I’d be grateful for Lunathrae’s gift too,” he said quietly after a moment, voice carrying an edge of awe. “Power like that… it shapes everything around it, whether you worship it or not.”

For a moment, his gaze met Vornakir’s—no comparison, just acknowledgment. A druid’s respect for something that had both built and outlasted empires.

Then he exhaled, the stillness loosening as he slung the folded blanket under one arm and found his grin again. “You’ll have to tell me that story again out there,” he said, tone brightening. “Preferably with sunlight and something to eat. So, we still doing this or what?”

Sol let out a soft, amused sigh, the corners of his mouth curving as Redd’s words settled into the air.

“Well,” he began, brushing invisible dust from his sleeve as if collecting his composure along with it, “before this turns into an impromptu philosophy class, I’d rather not waste what’s left of the golden hour.” His hand flicked lazily, and the picnic basket and art supply bag rose smoothly into his hands. A few stray sparkles of magic clung to the bags like sunlight caught in glass.

“I found a perfect spot near the College of Illusions,” he continued, turning toward the others. “It catches the afternoon light just right. And before anyone asks, yes, there’s enough space for both food *and* ego,” he flashed Vornakir a wink followed by an amused laugh.

He gestured toward the door, “Come on, before the light dies and I have to conjure our own.”

Shifting her gaze away from Vornakir, Zašia regarded the supplies Sol had packed for them and her lips pursed faintly. Conflict brewed inside of her. *Desperately*, she wanted connection, to feel included...

Only her mind's eye strayed to Caspian at that thought. She didn't feel *worthy* of that inclusion right now.

Breathing out a little sigh, she thought next on Vornakir's words. It must've been so much easier to say that no one would tell him who he could or could not pursue when the stakes with her were almost non-existent.

She smiled apologetically to the other aasimar. "I've got a lot of studying to do for midterms. You all go on and enjoy." The stroll to the cupboards resumed. "I'm good, really." And she'll be better when she finds the wine.

Redd lingered a few steps behind as Sunni’s magic caught the light, the sparkle of levitating baskets pulling a soft grin from him.

Then Zašia spoke. Her voice was calm, too calm, and he caught the faint tension in her shoulders before she turned toward the cupboards. Caspian didn’t look her way, and the silence between them said everything. Redd knew that kind of quiet—sharp-edged, born of pride and ache.

He hesitated, watching her move, and the memory came unbidden: fire-lit beasts of magma roaring from the dark, the smell of smoke, and the sick weight of believing he deserved it. He’d woken from that nightmare more than once to find Zašia there—steady, wordless, the calm that tethered him back to himself. Once, she had even stepped into the dream itself, her voice a stabilizing and motivating presence while his village burned around them. She’d always had that kind of steadiness—strong enough to keep others from falling apart, even when she didn’t feel steady herself.

He took a small step closer, voice quiet and meant only for her. “You know,” he said softly, “days like this… they help bring everyone back together. You’ve always been part of that. Even when you don’t feel it.”

A small pause, then gentler, almost warm: “And if you change your mind, you know where to find us.”

He lingered just long enough for the words to settle, then turned toward Sunni and the door. The grin returned, light but genuine. “Alright, sunshine—lead the way before Whimsy eats the snacks and crowns herself queen of the picnic.”

Those violet eyes snapped up to Redd and blinked rapidly, as if surprised to be spoken to. Gradually, that small wrinkle in her expression smoothed out again and she offered an even smaller smile, like a secret shared between the two of them, even if there was still uncertainty in her eyes. "Thank you."

More insidious than uncertainty, she held such doubt.

Zašia merely reiterated, with warmth she didn't feel suffusing her tone. "I'll see you all when you return, I'm sure."
